narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40—above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on profiles from thousands of past students. The model estimated a 56.1% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for rejection, as schools prioritize admitting those predicted to succeed and avoid high-risk profiles. The primary driver pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st, placing you in the bottom 10% of your peers that year—far below the average 5.53 decile—suggesting inconsistent academic performance later in your studies that strongly signaled risk to the model. This outweighed your undergraduate GPA, which had a notable positive influence by pulling the prediction toward passing, as your stronger college record indicated potential despite other weaknesses. Your Year 1 decile of 1st also contributed toward the failure prediction, reinforcing concerns about early struggles in a competitive school environment, even though it had less impact than your later performance. Compared to typical students, your LSAT score of 29.50 is well below the 36.70 average, and while your middle family income bracket aligns with many applicants, these factors played supporting roles behind the top academic deciles. Full-time enrollment plans, common for over 92% of students, didn't sway the outcome much here. The model saw your profile as riskier than most, where stronger decile rankings across years often tip predictions toward success. This rejection stings, especially with your above-average undergrad record showing real capability. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the average, strengthening your high school deciles through additional coursework or explanations in reapplications, and targeting schools with holistic reviews that value your full-time commitment and background. Gathering letters highlighting your resilience as a white male from a middle-income family could also help reframe your story for admissions teams. With targeted improvements, you can shift future predictions in your favor.
